crops.name: Cowpea (Beans)

forest.notes: Often planted late in the season after maize to avoid pest pressure

savanna.notes: Commonly intercropped with sorghum or millet

sahel.notes: A vital protein source, drought tolerant once established

soil.type: Sandy-loam soils with good drainage; tolerates poorer soils than maize
soil.testing_tip: Cowpea is good at fixing its own nitrogen, so it doesn't demand rich soil. Avoid waterlogged ground which causes root rot.

fertilizer.basal: Light NPK 15:15:15 at 50kg per hectare, avoid excess nitrogen which causes leafy growth at the expense of pods
fertilizer.topdress: Usually not needed if basal application is done correctly
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Cowpea improves soil for the next crop through nitrogen fixation - minimal external fertilizer needed

watering.critical_stages: Flowering and pod formation - water stress here causes flower drop and poor pod set

common_pests_diseases.name: Pod borer (Maruca)
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Spray neem extract at flowering, avoid planting near old cowpea fields

common_pests_diseases.name: Aphids
common_pests_diseases.sign: Clusters on young shoots, sticky residue on leaves
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Wash off with water, encourage ladybird beetles which prey on aphids

common_pests_diseases.name: Bacterial blight
common_pests_diseases.sign: Water-soaked spots on leaves turning brown
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Use clean seed, avoid working in fields when leaves are wet

harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Pods turn yellow/brown and dry, rattle when shaken
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Hand-pick dry pods in stages as they mature, sun-dry further before threshing by beating

beginner_tips: Great crop to rotate with cereals - it restores soil fertility for the next maize or sorghum crop
Harvest pods promptly once dry, leaving them too long in the field invites pod-sucking insects and weather damage
Avoid too much nitrogen fertilizer - cowpea makes its own through root nodules with soil bacteria

crops.name: Groundnut (Peanut)

savanna.notes: Major crop in savanna zones, plant with onset of consistent rains

sahel.notes: Drought-tolerant varieties available for shorter rain windows

soil.type: Light, sandy-loam soils - heavy clay makes pod and harvesting difficult
soil.testing_tip: Squeeze dry soil in your hand - if it crumbles easily and doesn't form hard clumps, it's suitable. Pods need to push into loose soil easily.

fertilizer.basal: Phosphorus-rich fertilizer (like SSP) at planting helps root and nodule development, around 50kg per hectare
fertilizer.topdress: Generally not needed - too much nitrogen reduces pod yield
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Wood ash applied to soil provides potassium and calcium which groundnut needs for good pod filling

watering.critical_stages: Flowering and pegging (when pods form and push into soil) - this is the most sensitive period to drought

common_pests_diseases.name: Groundnut rosette virus
common_pests_diseases.sign: Yellowing and stunting, spread by aphids
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Plant early/with the first rains, control aphid vectors, use resistant varieties

common_pests_diseases.name: Leaf spot diseases
common_pests_diseases.sign: Brown or dark spots on leaves leading to defoliation
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Crop rotation, avoid continuous groundnut on same plot

common_pests_diseases.name: Termites
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Avoid planting on old termite-infested land, remove crop residue that attracts termites

harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Leaves yellow, pods have developed visible veining and the inside of the shell darkens
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Loosen soil with a hoe or fork and pull up the whole plant, pods come up attached to roots, then dry and pick off

beginner_tips: Don't plant in heavy clay - pods struggle to develop and harvesting becomes very labor intensive
Like cowpea, groundnut fixes nitrogen and is excellent for soil improvement before a cereal crop
Dry harvested pods thoroughly before storage to prevent aflatoxin (a dangerous mold) - never store damp groundnuts

crops.name: Soybean

forest.notes: Needs reliable rainfall through the growing period

savanna.notes: Widely grown commercial crop in middle belt areas

soil.type: Well-drained loamy soils with good organic matter
soil.testing_tip: Soybean is sensitive to very acidic soils - if your land grows poor cassava or has reddish, very acidic-looking soil, consider liming before planting soybean

fertilizer.basal: Phosphorus fertilizer at planting (e.g. SSP 50kg/ha), inoculate seeds with Rhizobium bacteria if available for better nitrogen fixation
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Apply lime to acidic soils a few weeks before planting if soil test shows low pH

watering.frequency: Rain-fed, needs more consistent moisture than cowpea or groundnut

common_pests_diseases.name: Pod-sucking bugs
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Spray at pod formation stage if infestation is heavy, harvest promptly

common_pests_diseases.name: Rust
common_pests_diseases.sign: Small reddish-brown pustules on leaf undersides
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Use resistant varieties, avoid dense planting that traps humidity

harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Pods and leaves turn brown/yellow and most leaves drop
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Cut or pull whole plants, dry for several days, then thresh

beginner_tips: If available, treating seed with Rhizobium inoculant before planting drastically improves nitrogen fixation and yield - ask at agricultural input shops
Avoid harvesting too late - pods that over-dry in the field shatter and you lose seed on the ground
Good rotation crop before maize since it leaves nitrogen-enriched soil behind

crops.name: Bambara Groundnut

savanna.notes: A resilient traditional crop, often grown on marginal land

sahel.notes: One of the most drought-tolerant legumes, suitable for poor soils

soil.type: Tolerates poor, sandy, and even slightly degraded soils better than most legumes
soil.testing_tip: If groundnut struggles on a particular plot, bambara nut is a good alternative as it's even more tolerant of poor conditions

fertilizer.basal: Usually grown without fertilizer, light phosphorus application can help on very poor soils
fertilizer.organic_alternative: None needed typically - this crop is valued for thriving without inputs

watering.frequency: Rain-fed; extremely drought tolerant, can survive prolonged dry spells
watering.critical_stages: Flowering and pegging, similar to groundnut, though it tolerates stress better

common_pests_diseases.name: Termites
common_pests_diseases.sign: Damage to roots and pods underground
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Avoid termite-prone fields, remove old crop residue

common_pests_diseases.name: Leaf spot
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Generally minor, crop rotation helps prevent buildup

harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Leaves yellow and dry, pods firm underground
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Similar to groundnut - lift plants and pick pods from roots, then dry

beginner_tips: Excellent choice if you have poor, sandy land that struggles to grow other crops
Highly nutritious and an important food security crop, good for household consumption
Like groundnut, dry pods thoroughly before storage to avoid mold
